scouting_focus: Bleached spots expanding under extended leaf wetness
cultural_program: Maintain adequate but not excessive nitrogen to avoid chronically lean turf
Reduce dew and leaf wetness duration where possible
Improve air movement and surface drying
Avoid unnecessary drought stress

chemical_strategy.positioning: Use preventive or very early curative applications when pressure is predictable
chemical_strategy.rotation: Rotate FRAC groups and avoid repeated single-site overuse
notes: Tie fungicide timing to forecasted pressure and turf susceptibility
Do not rely on fungicides to compensate for chronically starved turf

scouting_focus: Rapid foliar blight under humid nights
cultural_program: Avoid pushing excessive succulent nitrogen during high-risk weather
Shorten leaf wetness where feasible
Improve airflow and drainage of persistently wet surfaces
Water deeply and at the right time rather than repeatedly rewetting the canopy

chemical_strategy.positioning: Preventive protection is stronger than chasing fully active outbreaks
chemical_strategy.rotation: Rotate effective modes of action and avoid leaning entirely on one chemistry family
notes: Foliar disease pressure rises when turf remains wet overnight

cultural_program: Reduce surface saturation and chronic wetness
Improve drainage and airflow
Avoid overwatering ahead of hot humid periods
Relieve sealing and compaction where they trap water

chemical_strategy.positioning: Use true Pythium-active materials preventively on high-risk sites
chemical_strategy.rotation: Stay within oomycete-active groups and avoid assuming broad-spectrum fungicides cover Pythium
notes: Pythium moves too fast for slow reaction on vulnerable surfaces
Water management is part of disease control

scouting_focus: Wilted rings in summer stress periods
Root decline before severe foliar collapse
cultural_program: Build deeper roots before summer through balanced spring management
Limit compaction and chronic saturation
Avoid severe drought stress on vulnerable turf
Use summer venting and moisture precision on high-risk surfaces

chemical_strategy.positioning: Preventive timing matters because visible summer symptoms start after root infection has advanced
chemical_strategy.rotation: Use labeled preventive materials and avoid waiting for rings to collapse before acting
notes: This is a root disease management problem as much as a spray problem
Programs should start before peak summer stress

scouting_focus: Yellowing and thinning in stressed areas
Recurring decline on weak traffic lines
cultural_program: Keep nutrition steady and avoid chronically lean greens
Manage traffic and mechanical stress
Avoid moisture extremes from both drought and oversaturation
Support recovery with balanced fertility and sensible rolling/mowing intensity

chemical_strategy.positioning: Chemistry works best when paired with stress reduction and nutrition correction
chemical_strategy.rotation: Rotate effective fungicide groups and do not assume chemistry alone will hold failing greens together
notes: Anthracnose pressure rises fast on weak Poa annua

scouting_focus: Localized moisture contrast inside and outside the ring
environmental_drivers: Organic food source in the profile
cultural_program: Use wetting agents where hydrophobicity is part of the symptom complex
Probe moisture in and around the ring
Vent or cultivate where sealing worsens moisture contrast
Hand water strategically instead of soaking the whole surface

chemical_strategy.positioning: Combine fungicide selection with soil-wetting and movement into the affected zone where labeled
chemical_strategy.rotation: Use effective materials as part of a full moisture-management response
notes: Fairy ring is often as much a water-behavior problem as a pathogen problem
Dry-ring symptoms can persist if only fungicides are used
